html:
YJ Link is not a NAND-handler story. It is a small Korean SMT automation equipment company being re-labeled by the market as a SpaceX, Tesla, robot, and reshoring stock. The prior thesis is directionally wrong on the business but right that the market can miss a sharp earnings inflection.

At KRW 8,600, the May 18 KRX close on Naver, YJ Link is worth about KRW 245 billion on 28.44 million shares. The April 2026 zero-coupon CB adds 3.96 million shares at KRW 6,308 if converted, so fully diluted equity value is closer to KRW 279 billion, partly offset by KRW 25 billion of growth cash. This is not statistically cheap on trailing numbers, since 2025 was a loss year. The opportunity is whether 2026 is the first year of a real platform transition rather than a one-quarter rebound.

The business is SMT line automation: PCB transfer equipment, laser tracking and marking, routers, IFCT inspection, smart-factory modules, storage automation, and AMR-related handling. The company’s own site calls it a “Global SMT Platform Leader” and shows products that sit around the SMT line rather than the chip test floor. That matters because the real driver is not NAND capex. It is the relocation and duplication of electronics manufacturing capacity into Mexico, India, Vietnam, and eventually Thailand, plus the upgrade from single tools to integrated SMT full-line supply.

The Q1 turn is already visible. The May 14 quarterly report summary showed revenue of KRW 14.7 billion, operating profit of KRW 1.1 billion, and net income of KRW 2.6 billion, versus KRW 7.6 billion, negative KRW 1.3 billion, and negative KRW 1.8 billion a year ago (AWAKE/DART summary). That is a clean profit inflection after 2025 revenue of KRW 46.1 billion and operating loss of KRW 3.5 billion. More important, Q1 was not the peak quarter: Hana Securities reported that April alone was already approaching 70% of 2Q 2025 revenue and that management’s 2026 guide is KRW 80 billion of revenue with 12% operating margin, followed by a KRW 100 billion 2027 revenue target (Hana report).

The variant perception is that investors are treating YJ Link as a Korean “Musk theme” stock, while the underlying business model is quietly changing from peripheral SMT equipment into globally localized full-line automation. The March 2026 SMT full-line contract with an automotive parts manufacturer was KRW 4.1 billion, 8.5% of 2025 revenue, and runs through June 2027 (AWAKE/DART summary). In June 2025, the company disclosed a Foxconn India PCB transfer equipment order of about $1.67 million, tied to a line connecting more than 30 SMT and inspection machines, with warranty and parts revenue potential (Maeil). In August 2025, it completed a 7,272 square meter Mexico plant with capacity for 300 SMT machines per month, taking group SMT capacity to roughly 1,000 units per month across Mexico, Vietnam, and Korea (Maeil).

That footprint is the strategic asset. Customers are pushing electronics assembly out of China and closer to end markets. YJ Link can sell to Foxconn in India, automotive suppliers in Korea, Tesla or SpaceX-linked manufacturing ecosystems in North America, and future India/Mexico expansions without shipping every tool from Korea. For an SMT customer, the equipment decision is not only price. It is delivery speed, uptime, onsite service, and the ability to replicate a qualified line in multiple countries. That is where a small Korean company can become more valuable than a generic hardware vendor.

The robot and SpaceX angle is real enough to be an option, but dangerous if it becomes the entire thesis. Leading Investment Securities expects 2026 revenue of KRW 80.7 billion and operating profit of KRW 8.2 billion, and specifically framed North American robot value-chain entry as the re-rating trigger (MoneyToday/Daum). YJ Link has disclosed or been reported as supplying Tesla, SpaceX, Harman, Foxconn, and automotive electronics customers, but a confirmed Optimus-scale order is not yet in the numbers. The upside case is not that YJ Link is “a SpaceX stock.” It is that a company already serving high-end electronics manufacturing could become a repeat automation vendor as robotics, aerospace electronics, EV modules, and AI infrastructure hardware require more localized PCB assembly lines.

Retail context is very hot. Naver’s board today is dominated by SpaceX, IR-news demands, and investment-warning chatter. KRX designated the stock as an investment-warning name after the price more than doubled versus 15 trading days earlier (KRX notice). That is a near-term volatility risk, but it also means domestic retail has discovered the story before institutions have built real coverage. Foreign ownership is still only 2.34% on Naver. If Q2 and Q3 numbers validate the guide, the shareholder base can rotate from theme traders into small-cap growth funds.

The valuation is a knife edge. On the 2026 guide, KRW 8.2 to 9.6 billion of operating profit probably means KRW 7 to 9 billion of normalized net income, so the stock is around 27 to 35x fully diluted 2026 earnings. That is not deep value. But if revenue reaches KRW 110 billion in 2027 at 12 to 13% margin, EPS can move toward KRW 330 to 380 fully diluted. If full-line orders, Mexico utilization, India conversion, and robot/aerospace programs push revenue to KRW 150 to 180 billion at mid-teens margins, EPS can exceed KRW 600. At that point today’s price is only the opening multiple on a company that may have gone from loss-making niche supplier to global SMT automation platform.

The biggest risk is that the stock is ahead of fundamentals. The CB is 12% dilutive, 2025 free cash flow was deeply negative due to capex, and the company is now executing several overseas expansions at once. If Q2 revenue does not step up materially from Q1, the “April acceleration” story weakens. If the Foxconn, Tesla, SpaceX, and robot references do not convert into repeat orders, YJ Link is just an expensive small equipment cyclical. The balance sheet is not a net-cash floor story. The downside floor comes from real factories, a founder group still around 43%, global customer references, and a business that has already returned to profit, not from idle cash.

My base case is that 2026 revenue lands near KRW 85 billion, operating profit near KRW 9 billion, and 2027 revenue reaches KRW 115 billion as full-line and Mexico/India demand scale. That supports a stock around KRW 13,000, roughly 51% upside from KRW 8,600. The bear case is KRW 4,800, down 44%, if 2026 revenue stalls below KRW 65 billion, the CB overhang dominates, and the SpaceX/robot premium unwinds. The bull case is KRW 25,000, up 191%, if revenue ramps toward KRW 160 to 180 billion with 15% operating margins and the market starts valuing YJ Link as a global automation platform with a credible humanoid, aerospace, and EV electronics order pipeline.
